dietary_typical.description: Typical dietary intake from rosemary/lemon balm/perilla seasoning
dietary_typical.rationale: Rosemary 1-2% RA by dry weight; lemon balm ~1%; perilla ~3%. A typical culinary serving (1-5 g dry herb) yields 5-50 mg RA. Source: comp-020 wiki-archive.md L148 (reviews PMC9143754, PMC8989115).

supplement_lemon_balm.description: Single 500 mg Melissa officinalis extract (lemon-balm-as-RA) standardized to RA
supplement_lemon_balm.study: Noguchi-Shinohara M et al. (cited in Kang 2021 PMC7828042 L14)
supplement_lemon_balm.rationale: Reference human study; fed/fasted state evaluated.

supplement_perilla.description: Single Perilla frutescens extract (200 mg as RA) human single-dose
supplement_perilla.study: Baba S et al. 2004 (PMID 15120569) Life Sci 75:165-78
supplement_perilla.verification: Quoted verbatim in Kang 2021 PMC7828042 L14: 'plasma concentrations of the parent rosmarinic acid and conjugated rosmarinic acid in human subjects were reported about 20 nM and 1200 nM, respectively, at 1 h after oral intake of the Perilla frutescens extract.'

rat_oral_bioavailability_absolute.study: Wang J et al. 2017 (RSC Adv 7:9057-63, DOI 10.1039/C6RA28237G), dose range 12.5-50 mg/kg in rats
rat_oral_bioavailability_absolute.verification: Quoted verbatim in Kang 2021 PMC7828042 L14 + L59: 'In the dose range of 12.5-50 mg/kg, the absolute bioavailability of rosmarinic acid was 0.9-1.7% in rats, and their AUC did not show dose proportionality.'

plasma_protein_binding.source: Kang 2021 PMC7828042 L25, L69; rat plasma; unpublished data referenced
plasma_protein_binding.implication: Of total plasma RA, only ~8.6% is free and capable of binding C3 convertase.

gut_luminal_concentration.source: Kang 2021 PMC7828042 L15: 'rosmarinic acid concentrations in the intestine following the oral administration of rosmarinic acid (200 mg) could be calculated as 252-1100 μM, which can plausibly induce drug interactions.'
gut_luminal_concentration.implication: At dietary-tier or supplement-tier oral doses, gut-luminal RA concentrations transiently reach the IC50 range (5-180 μM) and well exceed the C3b covalent IC50 (34 μM).

gi_stability_loss.source: Veras 2022 PMC9784852 L19; gastric stability >98%, intestinal phase recovery 69-75% due to alkali-pH instability
gi_stability_loss.implication: RA is reasonably stable through gastric transit; ~25-30% degraded by intestinal alkaline conditions; remainder available for gut-luminal complement interaction or absorption.

modeling_choice: For fluid-phase / systemic prediction, use the Baba 2004 human Cmax: free RA ~20 nM (= 0.020 μM), conjugated ~1200 nM at Tmax. Note: conjugated metabolites are NOT free RA; they are likely inactive at C3 convertase covalent modification (RA's mechanism requires the free catechol). For gut-luminal prediction, use the 252-1100 μM intestinal concentration range. The two scales are 4-5 orders of magnitude apart — confirming the wiki's claim that RA operates at different geometric scales (systemic vs. gut-luminal).
